So in client attraction, we're really dealing with three issues. But we're going to move on to the first one soon, but not immediately, because it's actually the least difficult of all three. So one is opportunity, right? Where are they? What are the sources of clients? How can we get to them, access, and clarity about who we ought to go after that we should be matching ourselves up with? Because your client that's best for you is probably not the client that's best for me, vice versa. So opportunity is one set of issues. Methodology is the second set of issues. How do we position ourselves? Then how do we market to these people ourselves? How do we actually sell to them once we've got a situation with them? And what we've been talking about all morning, how do we manage them once we get them? The third thing then is really ideology. And that's the least concrete practical thing we can touch. And yet it has as much to do about our success in getting clients as really anything. So there is the belief system you have about yourself, right? And that gets into what am I worth? What should I be getting paid? How adept am I? How skilled am I? How ready am I? How qualified am I? That belief system. Then there's a belief system about clients, okay? Are they all cheap? Are they all dumb? Are they difficult to get? The belief system we have about clients. There's the belief system we have about the business in general. And there's a belief system everybody has about money. The latter, by the way, which we'll talk about a little bit tonight in a bonus session. Most people's beliefs about money are unhelpful rather than helpful because most of them have been taught to you and implanted in you by people who don't have a good relationship with money themselves. And so you got what you get, right? So all of these are important. So if you look at page 102, here's one of the biggies. How dare you consult, write copy form. Clients who are a lot more successful than you are. So like one of my very good clients, been with them for many years, almost since the beginning. So Greg Ranker and Bill Guthe, Guthe Ranker, the company does about now about a billion a year. Not even as a couple, divided. They each have a lot more money than I got. They build an enormously successful company. So how dare I tell them what to do and take money from them. So this is really in the way of a lot of people. So first, you never want to underestimate the stupidity of the successful. So one of the richest clients I have, last year, twice at my house, he walked through the front door. He walked through the screen door once and ripped the whole screen out of it. And in the winter, he walked through the plexiglass and knocked all the glass out of the door. I mean, he's just easily distracted. The secret reason why there aren't more rich people is because poor people think there's something special about rich people that they don't have. If you took all the poor people and had them go spend a week with rich people, there'd be more rich people because they'd walk out of the room at the end of the week saying, I can't even believe these people are as dumb as they are. We got a guy right now to track. You'll probably meet him. His nickname is Frog. A heavy-set guy. So Frog, I know he didn't get through high school. I don't even know if he got through junior high school. And Frog doesn't make much money. And Frog's pretty simple. However, Frog's smarter than some of my clients. The main reason why Frog doesn't have a lot of money, there's two reasons. One, he doesn't care. But beyond that, he thinks there's something just unique about rich people, some gene they have that he doesn't have. He has no idea they're as dumb as they are. If he knew, he might do something about it. So you don't want to underestimate the stupidity as, you know, the successful. They're not as bright as everybody thinks they are, not even close. Secondly, there's an absence of specialized knowledge and marketing knowledge. To be fair, nobody's good at everything. So again, the CEO of Weight Watchers, I'm sure, is very good at being a CEO, but you don't know how to get a fat person to a meeting. So specialized knowledge is really the issue here. So if we jump for a second. So here's what I was for. So presidents are dependent on people who are presidents to become presidents. So what Carville enrolled, one's the satanic opposite of the other politically. So what Carville enrolled know how to do is direct marketing. They understand demographics, they understand lists, they understand direct mail, they understand how to do direct marketing. The presidents that they made possible probably can't even spell direct marketing. So who's more valuable to who? It's a guy by the name of Ken Cragan. If you don't know him, Ken Cragan had a big career, has a talent manager, and neither Kenny Rogers nor the Smothers Brothers would exist without Ken Cragan. My guess is, certainly Kenny made more money than Craig did, and my guess is the Smothers Brothers made more money. So Donald Trump, I assure you he's richer than Burnett or his chief negotiator and lawyer George Roth. But he ain't possible without George Roth. George has negotiated, done all the contract work, closed every deal from beginning to now. You see him a little bit now because he's on the celebrity apprentice every once in a while, but generally speaking he's invisible, but he's pretty important. So here's this list. So Arthur Knight is the guy that keeps my collector cards running. I'm worth a lot more than Arthur, but I pay him what he demands because I can't... Ignition key radio, that's the extent of my automotive knowledge. And I'm now up to four cards, three of which are old. So I need this guy. Jim Welch, he works for us at the track, but he's also a carpenter, handyman, whatever. So his key to my house and he makes the house work, because I don't know how to do that either. So if something goes wrong, I call him and he comes over, but mostly he prevents anything from going wrong. So he comes in, he changes light bulbs, he takes the lint out of the dryer, he tightens the hinges, every song, he puts the patio furniture away in the fall, gets it out in the spring, does the deck, I pay him whatever he asks. In his case, he refuses to itemize a bill, every year he just gives me a slip of paper with a number written on it. I don't... I mean, he goes and buys stuff, I have no idea. I don't know, I don't care. Because what am I going to do without the guy? I don't know how to do any of that stuff. I have no earthly idea how to get the lint out of the dryer, nor incidentally do I care to know. I can't hang a picture straight. So every so often, there's a bunch of wind pictures piled up and he comes over and puts them up. I don't have to tell him, he just comes and does it, and at the end of the year he gives me a piece of paper and I write him a check. Now, he can't possibly be close to my network. I know he doesn't make an income I make, but that is irrelevant in our relationship and in my willing to pay him whatever he asks. We shall not tell him, but had the number on the piece of paper last year been double what the number was, I wouldn't have said a word. He doesn't know that and there's no point in telling him. And he probably won't be there tomorrow night. Darren Garmin, now we're climbing up the ladder a little bit here, but so Darren handles real estate investments in mind in Iowa. Still, I'm richer than Darren and I make more money than Darren, but I can't read a real estate contract to save my life, nor do I wish to. I don't want to keep track of when we need to do a 1031 exchange, I don't want to run up. So, I don't question and his relationship to me in net worth or income is irrelevant in our relationship or my willingness to pay him. I won't believe you the point. I'll just say people, so Daniel Wiggins manages my pension accounts. Tracy Monroe is my accountant, two literary agents. Amo Judd is the guy that owns the charter company that flies me around. Claire Umhoalts, my horse trainer. I guarantee you, I got more money than any of them. Maybe a few are mad at together. That's not relevant in our relationship or what I am willing to pay them because they have specialized knowledge and specialized skill that I need. So big key here is making the client understand the specialized nature of what it is that you do. We'll talk about positioning. I mean, we'll get to third, consulting is about much more than who has the bigger wallet and everything attached to consulting is about much more than who has the bigger wallet. So there's a lot of value you might deliver in a relationship that has absolutely nothing to do with the income differential between you and them. So one is simple, fresh eyes. The classic too close to the trees to see the forest and vice versa. I forget how that goes, but there's actually a term for it. It's called skatoma. Almost every business owner has it. You should look it up because it's a good rap to give them. Part of your diagnosis is you have skatoma. I do. Yeah, it's glowing and dark if you don't fix it. So just fresh eyes. You see things they now no longer see because they become immune to it just like we become immune to spells in our house. So if you're a cat owner, you don't smell them anymore. Somebody fresh comes in. I mean, they smell in a hot second. If you're a cigarette, where was our cigarette smoker? You say, okay, so our cigarette smoker, right? It's in your clothes. It's in the furniture. It's in the car seat and you don't notice it and she probably doesn't notice anymore either. Okay, see, yeah. Well, see my parents, my parents spoke about people that track spoke, but my parents spoke about it all the time. It doesn't bother me because I grew up with it. So I can be sitting right next to a smoker. It doesn't bother me. But somebody walking in your house who's a non-smoker, they know in a, I mean, you know, so we become immune to that. Well, we visually become immune to what's going on in our business too. We just don't notice stuff. And so fresh eyes is a big issue. They don't notice that they haven't updated that brochure in 18 years and the photographs and it looked like they're from the 1950s. They don't notice because that's their thing and they're immune to it. Dumb questions. The asking of dumb questions is enormously valuable. Not just smart questions, dumb questions. Hardly anybody gets asked, well, why do you do that? Well, why do you do that? Well, why do you, by the time you get to the third, why do you do that? By the way, most of the time they can't defend what it is they do. They don't know. I don't know why we do that. You know, Zig's story about the ham? How many don't know the story about the ham? Raise your hands if you don't know the story. I got to do it because it's useful to you. So you want to memorize it. You want to be able to tell it. I'll just tell the short version. You go, then go build an embellished version. So it may not be Zig's, but Zig told it enough that he did it. So the story about the ham is that the daughter now is making holiday dinner for the first time, and everybody's going to be coming over. And she is getting ready to cut the ends off the ham to put it in the pot. And people there object, why would you cut the ends off? And she says, well, you always cut the ends off. Well, what do you mean you always cut, well, mom always cut the ends off. Well, that doesn't make any sense. So they get on the phone and they call mom. You know, why do you cut the ends off? And she says, you always cut the ends off. Well, why do you cut the ends off? Well, because grandma always cut the ends off. So they get on a phone and they call grandma. Why do you cut the ends off the ham? Which is I cut the ends off the ham because I only had one small pot and the thing wouldn't fit in it. I don't know why anybody else cuts the ends off the ham. So for two generations, everybody's cutting the ends off the ham because that's what we do. Well, there's a lot of that in business. So if you ask like three dumb questions in a row about anything, you'll get to the point they can't defend it. And then that's kind of interesting because there may be opportunity there to do something better. For thought, just making somebody slow down and think about their business. The questionnaire. If you make them fill out the questionnaire and it's a really good questionnaire, many will say to you, just filling out the questionnaire was valuable because it made me think about what I'm doing in my business. They don't stop and think. They shop every day and they're just doing. So forced thought, their interaction with you, make them defend ideas. So as I said, every business and every business owner has this secular religion that they believe about their business. Works just like a real religion. It's got 10 commandments that can't be broken. It's got the stuff like you can't eat fish on Friday or you'll go to hell until we change your mind and then you can't eat fish on Friday, but you accept during the length. And if you're Amish, you can use a cell phone in your business, but you can't bring it in the house. And so businesses have the same crap. They all have the same pile of articles of faith that they believe about their business, their products, their services, their prices, their customers. They haven't been tested in eons. If they have ever been tested, they're not fact-based. They're not data-based. They're not experience-based. They're just a belief system. So how do you know that? So they tell you X. Our customers won't spend more than this for that. How do you know? Well, that's, I know. Really? When's the last time you price tested? We've never priced tested. So you don't know squat, right? That's what you don't know. You believe, but you don't know nothing. You don't know anything. Well, they would never do that. Why would they never do that? Why would they never do that? Oh, no. Let's ask them. Let's find out. On Earth, ignore neglected opportunities. Again, you can bring a strategy box to the table that's different than their strategy. Find the undervalued story. This is big. Almost everybody, every business, and certainly every business owner, somewhere has buried. Usually it's personal. There's or someone's a great story that they're not using because mostly they focus on product. They don't focus on personal. So and Miracle Air, for example. They didn't give it to me. I defined it. It turns out that company, let me see if I have the piece. I don't know if I brought the piece or not. Yeah. So I could be able to see it real well, but here's this picture. It's this guy. It's a World War II fighter pilot guy. World War II Garbany's next to a World War II Air point. This is the founder of the company. This guy, I mean, it's incredible. He's, you know, just read your couple lines. My name is Ken Dahlborker. World War II I served in the Army Air Corps as a fighter pilot. On August 16, 1944, I had just seven other P-51 Mustang pilots, fought 80 German planes near Drough, France. I shot down four Messerschmitts before I was shot down. A French family hid me and I made an escape on a bicycle right past enemy forces to re-jure in my squadron. I was shot down a second time on December 28, 1944 during the Battle of the Bulge. And on February 14, 1945, I was shot down for a third and last time. I was wounded on land and couldn't escape and ended up a POW, installing number seven until the end of the war. In total, I flew 103 missions and I'm a triple A's. Having shot down 15 enemy aircraft, I received the Distinguished Service Cross Silver Star, Bronze Star, Purple Heart, Heart and other awards. Now the average customer here is 60 years plus. It's binary. These guys, John Wayne, Audie Murphy, they ain't using them. They don't even tell me the guy exists because why is that important? Right? This, I mean, go hire a celebrity. We got a celebrity and they got him buried. They don't even know he's there and it has any value. I don't find him. He tells me about him. Really is it oh by the way? Not because he doesn't think it's anything either. But at least he tells me. I'm going, what? What is this guy alive? He's alive. Is he lucid? Yep. He's alive and lucid. He still has got four companies. He buys this one back every time the big company screws it up, fixes it, sells it again. So now I go back to the management. I say, are you guys okay with him? Oh, yeah. He gets a royalty on every sale. Why aren't you using him? How would we use him? I say, well, that's my story. That's my guy. Because now I use him because he wears one to sell guys on the idea that it's not lopping off your manhood to wear a hearing aid. If it's good enough for me, John Wayne, it's okay for you. He's like gold this guy. I find it again and again and again and again. They don't know they got it. They don't know it's important. They don't know any of that. So stories, validation. Sometimes it's just validating for somebody that what they are doing is smart, right? Clever. This ought to stay in place. Motivation. Hey, owners of businesses need motivated too. Who motivates the motivator? Who's telling them? Great job. Go get them. Nobody. Accountability. A lot of clients need to be held accountable for implementation. Nobody holds them accountable for implementation. It's a value. Help with decision making, implementation or accelerated implementation. Maybe they get it done without you, but they'll get it done faster with you. Resources and connections. So all of those are values that you bring to the table that have nothing to do with the relationship between your income, their income, your net worth, their net worth. Why do people ask others for advice? All sorts of reasons. All sorts. They're categories on your page 105. So sometimes they just want sounding board. Sometimes they're looking for shortcuts. Again, they can get it done without you, but they'll get there quicker with you. Now why do clients hire copywriters? Basically, the same reasons. So A, they have a real need and they can't do it themselves. And they know that if they do it themselves, they'll muck it up. So even if they have big in-house, so my big, so Guthrie Ranker has all kinds of in-house marketing personnel. Why they got me working on a project. Because if they do it in-house, they're going to get the same stuff they already got. It'll be derivative of derivative of derivative. I did a bunch of work years ago for Amway Corporation and we sat in the boardroom and we were talking about doing the infomercial part of it, the video part of it. Well they got studios up there that'll make Hollywood look sick. I mean they got great facilities and the one piece is none enough. We want you to do it all outside. Because I was talking about going up there and students were, why do you want me to do it all outside? He says because if we do it, we do any part of it. Well Amway eyes it and we'll be right back where we started from. Now he gets like the gold star for knowing. But so they need you to do it because they can't do it themselves, right? Another one is they can't do it as well or they can't do it as quickly. Special purpose. They've got a crisis, they've got an opportunity, whatever, to implement strategic plans. So if you bring them strategy they don't already have or aren't already using, you get to create work for yourself. What do you need to capitalize on this? You only need more know how skill and available time than they've got. The punchline of that joke is I don't need to outrun the bear, I just need to outrun you. If you don't know the joke, somebody can give it to you on a break. I'm time pressed. So you only need more, so John Francis Tides quote, if you don't have this, you should like write it on your wall somewhere. It is in the land of the blind, the one eyed man is king. You don't even need both eyes if your client is half blind. So you only need more than they got and you need enough vision to identify opportunities that they haven't seen. That's the deal. So you don't, none of this has any relationship to I'm successful, they're more successful than I am, etc. I'm going to hold this maybe for later. So let's go to position. So let's jump to your page 114. Actually go so you can jump over to 115. There's two things here I want to give you a use old OB as an example, but it wouldn't matter if we used others. So if you look at contemporary top copywriters, you look at great advertising people who built great ad businesses. So it doesn't matter if you look at how much you look at bedside or you look at Carlton on down the list or you look at over the but I'll use over the is an example. So there's two things in this right up about over the critically important. The first one, I think I underlined them. Yeah. So the first one is the statement above all other advertising people. The advertised Ogilvy. You got to get this. You got to be in the business of advertising you. Not just your services. All services, all services can be commoditized. The distinction in the differentiation has to be you. So everything we're going to talk about in positioning has that as a consideration. He was as much in the business as much in the business of being Ogilvy has in the business of being anything else. Not in advertising, but if you look at Trump, see Trump anybody thinks Trump's in the real estate business is like 10 years behind the times. You're not in a real estate business. He's in the Trump business. It's a better business. So you got to be in the you business as much as you are in the writing business because the point of differentiation is all you. This is extremely uncomfortable for a lot of people. I get that because I didn't like it at all. My preference would actually be probably many of your preferences. My preference originally would be to be invisible, in oblivion. Nobody knows who I am. I work in isolation and I get a lot of money handed to me through a slot for my work. That would be my preference. I don't like him. My picture splattered all over everything. I don't like all the self-aggrandizement. I'm over it now. But I mean for a long time, I didn't like any of it. I would much rather third party sell the first party sell, meaning I'd rather sell somebody else than sell me. The problem is that I don't get all the money. So I abandoned that fairly early. But by preference, I'd rather do it. So I get it. I understand probably more than you can imagine. What works is self-promotion, self-marketing, self-advertising, making it all about you. And so I would get that. Now, gee, it seems like it's all about you. Yep, you're right. Because that's where the money comes from. So jump to 116 and let me talk about a clump of information. So in the beginning, so now for your beginners. In the beginning, you want to become known for something to a chosen market, which is why it's important to narrow the field of who it is you're going to market for something. You've got to get known for something. And it's easiest to begin narrow and then go broad, that it is to begin broad and go narrow because you get lost when you're broad. So if you look at the next page, so here's sort of my path. So I started early by positioning myself as somebody who did direct response marketing. I understand direct response marketing. I'm a specialist in direct response marketing. And so I can get results. So broad would be now direct marketing for any business or sales problem. But immediately I also went narrow. And so I lived in two niche markets. I sold to other speakers. So to my peers at the time about 6,000 or so reachable in that universe. And so direct response marketing for speakers. But digs sell product. I'm the guy. I understand it better than anybody else. And I went to chiropractors. I backed into that by accident. It's really not important how I got there at this point. I'm not a chiropractor, never been a chiropractor. I can do everything but give an adjustment. To the right person under the right circumstances, I can give an adjustment. But I went there now as a direct response marketer to put patients in the doors of offices. Significantly, this group really paid no attention to my broad existence. Most didn't know. Didn't care. They also didn't pay any attention to my marketing of myself to speak vice versa. Most of them, unless they happen to speak to chiropractors, had no earthly idea I was over there doing what I was doing. To this day, three years ago I was at Parker, which is a big chiropractic convention. Even though we made no attempt to convince people this, we made an attempt to convince them of the opposite. People are coming up, Dr. Kennedy, haven't seen you in a long time. Because we did such a good job talking to them in their language, they just have in their head that I'm a chiropractor. So let's stay over there for a second. So from there, the commonality is putting the patient in the office. So it really doesn't matter now what kind of office we're putting the patient in.
